front: Ruler: first emperor of the Yuan dynasty, grandson of Genghis Khan and son of Genghis's youngest son, Tolui
Ruler: held court at a garden palace called Shangdu, known to Europeans as "Xanadu"
Ruler: hosted the Venetian traveler Marco Polo at his court
War: fought and defeated his younger brother Ariq Boke for the Mongol throne after their brother Mongke Khan died suddenly without an heir
War: declared a rival kurultai at Kaiping against his brother's kurultai at Karakorum
War: after winning, ordered the commander Liu Bingzhong to build a new capital at Yanjing
War: styled himself "Zhongtong" ("the moderate ruler") and promised tax cuts to win local Chinese support
War: his brother's ally Alghu betrayed him after a defeat at Shimultai, slaughtering the tax collectors sent to his camp
War: the western Mongol khanates were kept from intervening by a separate war between Berke and Hulagu
Ruler: after winning the Toluid Civil War, became bogged down in a decades-long war with his nephew Kaidu
War: completed the Mongol conquest of China, absorbing the Southern Song after earlier conquests of Xi Xia and the Jin dynasty
War: the campaign's final push against the Southern Song was completed by his general Bayan
War: the Southern Song general Lu Wenhuan surrendered to him after the 1273 Battle of Xiangyang
War: an attempted alliance against the campaign used the warning "when the lips are gone, the teeth will feel the cold"
War: the advisor Yelu Chucai urged mercy during the campaign and was appointed head of the Central Secretariat
War: the naval Battle of Jiaoshan occurred late in the campaign, shortly before the regent Xie took power following the death of the Song emperor Duzong
Battle: 1279 naval battle that concluded the Mongol conquest of China; his fleet, though outnumbered ten-to-one off Guangdong, crushed the last Song resistance
back: Battle of Yaishan / Battle of Yamen (Kublai Khan)

front: Ruler: ordered two failed invasions of Japan; both fleets were destroyed by typhoons the Japanese called "divine wind" (kamikaze)
Ruler: after annexing Korea's Goryeo Kingdom, used Korean shipbuilders to prepare his invasion fleet against Japan
Ruler: to overcome fear of him before the invasion, a Japanese leader was told by his religious adviser Mugaku Sogen to scream "victory" until his fear subsided
Ruler: his fleet was defeated at the Battle of Bach Dang by Vietnam's Tran dynasty
Ruler: the Tran dynasty and the Champa kingdoms later accepted his supremacy over Vietnam
Ruler: a punitive naval expedition he sent to Java was repulsed after Raden Wijaya double-crossed the Mongol forces, indirectly enabling the founding of the Majapahit Empire (1293)
Ruler: his conquest of Burma's Pagan Kingdom caused its collapse, leaving the Shan peoples greater regional autonomy
Ruler: handscrolls illustrating his armies in battle depict "thunder crash bombs," an early form of explosive shell launched from catapults
Ruler: divided his subjects into a four-tier caste system, with a "semu" caste of foreigners ranked above Han Chinese
Ruler: commissioned the creation of the Phags-pa script
Ruler: converted Yunnan's conquered Dali Kingdom into a "tusi" chiefdom, a system of hereditary indirect rule
Ruler: after learning that his minister Ahmad Fanakati kept human skins in his closet, ordered the man's corpse thrown to dogs
Figure: dictated an embellished account of his travels through Kublai Khan's empire to a fellow prisoner, Rustichello da Pisa, while jailed in Genoa
Work: novel imagining a dialogue between Marco Polo and Kublai Khan, inspired by the embellished stories of their encounters
